feedback.text: I would want video game publishers to not be allowed to destroy their video games when they end support. They should have an end of life plan, which means to make the video game in a functional and playable state when support from the publisher ends. I believe that this closely aligns with the Digital Fairness Act's goals. There is a European Citizens' Initiative initiative called "Stop Destroying Videogames", which wants to do the same thing. It has over 1.4 million signatures from European Union citizens. I want this because I want to be able to play the video games that I bought. I want to own what I buy, not lose access to it at any time. I would also prefer not having to pay more money to cancel an online or digital subscription. I already paid when I first started the subscription. I don't see the point in paying even more money to cancel. I believe this also aligns with the Digital Fairness Act's goals. I also don't want to lose access to video games I bought, even if the video game hasn't lost support from the publisher. This includes things such as buying a license to a video game, that can be revoked at any time by the publisher or any other party. This wouldn't be for video games that everyone lost access to, but for video games removed from a specific person or persons. I expect to keep things that I buy, not have them removed for arbitrary reasons. This is kind of like the end of life plan proposal, but for video games that still have support from the publisher. I believe this also aligns with the Digital Fairness Act's goals. I also do not want video games, that either cost money or are free, to lock core features of the video game behind a pay wall, like microtransactions. Such as, if you paid money for a video game, open it, and find that you cannot access most or all of the video game, because it requires additional payments to function or be playable. I believe this to be deceptive and unfair. You might have seen the microtransactions before buying the video game, but you didn't know that access to most or all of the video game requires those additional payments. You thought the price of the video game was good enough for you and you bought the video game. After buying the video game, you realize that there was an additional price you had to pay to access most or all of the video game. It's deceptive because there is an additional price that isn't originally disclosed on the store page, but under the microtransaction or downloadable content section. This makes you believe the video game is cheaper than it actually is, which is deceptive. That's if you want to access most or all of the video game, which is something I would want to. If you download a seemingly free video game, called "free to play", but it requires additional payments to access, then that's deceptive. You downloaded the video game under the belief that it was free, but it actually costs money to access most or all of the video game. This pushes you into paying, if you want to access and play the video game. I believe that this also aligns with the Digital Fairness Act's goals.
